questions.questionText: The most common risk factor for primary open-angle glaucoma is:
questions.explanation: Raised intraocular pressure is the major modifiable risk factor for primary open-angle glaucoma, although not all patients have markedly elevated pressure. Younger age is not a risk factor. Hyperopia is more associated with angle-closure glaucoma. Corneal ulcer is unrelated.

questions.questionText: Halos around lights with severe ocular pain and mid-dilated fixed pupil suggest:
questions.explanation: Acute angle-closure glaucoma presents with sudden painful red eye, halos, corneal edema, and a mid-dilated fixed pupil. CRAO presents with sudden painless vision loss. Uveitis causes ciliary congestion and small irregular pupil. Open-angle glaucoma is usually chronic and asymptomatic early on.

questions.questionText: The initial drug commonly used to rapidly reduce intraocular pressure in acute angle closure is:
questions.optionD: Pilocarpine as sole first step before pressure reduction
questions.explanation: Acetazolamide is commonly used early to reduce aqueous humor formation and lower pressure in acute angle closure. Atropine and tropicamide worsen angle closure by causing mydriasis. Pilocarpine is used after some pressure reduction because an ischemic iris may initially respond poorly. This sequencing is a common exam trap.
questions.source: Parsons’ Diseases of the Eye, Glaucoma
